subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 13: Measure! In this unit you will learn how to measure the world around you. Measuring means finding out how long, how heavy, or how much something is, using numbers and units that everyone understands. You measure things far more often than you might think. When Amina helps her mother pour milk for the family tea, when Yusuf checks how tall he has grown, or when Halima weighs out rice at the Hargeisa market, they are all measuring.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Measure length using millimetres, centimetres and metres, and read a ruler correctly.
Measure mass using grams and kilograms, and read a scale.
Measure capacity using millilitres and litres, and read a measuring jug.
Estimate a measurement first, then measure carefully to check.
Convert between units: mm and cm, cm and m, g and kg, ml and l.
Choose the most sensible unit for measuring different objects.
Solve real-world measurement problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
Compare and order objects by their length, mass or capacity.

concepts.title: Measuring Length
concepts.explanation: Length tells us how long, how tall, or how far something is. To measure length we use a family of units that all belong to the metric system. The three you will use most in Year 3 are the millimetre, the centimetre and the metre. Millimetres (mm) measure very small lengths – the thickness of a coin, or the width of a pencil lead.
concepts.example: Centimetres (cm) measure small lengths – the width of your finger, or the length of a paper clip.

concepts.title: Measuring Mass
concepts.explanation: Mass tells us how heavy something is – how much matter, or ‘stuff’, it contains. In everyday speech people often say ‘weight’, and for our purposes the two feel the same, but the proper mathematical word is mass. To measure mass we again use metric units. In Year 3 the two you will use most are the gram and the kilogram. Grams (g) measure light objects – a paper clip, a coin, a single date.
concepts.example: Kilograms (kg) measure heavier objects – a book, a bag of rice, or a person.

concepts.title: Measuring Capacity
concepts.explanation: Capacity tells us how much liquid a container can hold. When Hassan fills a jug with water from the well, the amount of water the jug can hold is its capacity. To measure capacity we use metric units once more. In Year 3 the two you will use most are the millilitre and the litre. Millilitres (ml) measure small amounts – a teaspoon of medicine, or a small cup of milk.
concepts.example: Litres (l) measure larger amounts – a bottle of water, a jug of camel milk, or a bucket.

concepts.title: Estimating Before You Measure
concepts.explanation: An estimate is a sensible guess you make before you measure carefully. Good mathematicians estimate first out of habit, because an estimate warns you when a measurement has gone badly wrong. If Idris estimates that a table is about 1 metre long, and then his measurement comes out as 10 metres, he knows at once that he has made a mistake, because no ordinary table is as long as a bus. How do you make a good estimate? The trick is to compare with something you already know well. You know your own hand is about 8 cm…
concepts.example: Estimating and measuring work together like two hands: one guesses, the other checks. After you measure, always compare your answer to your estimate. If they are close, you can trust your work. If they are far apart, do…

concepts.title: Converting Between Units
concepts.explanation: Sometimes a measurement is given in one unit, but we need it in another. Changing a measurement from one unit to another is called converting. The whole idea rests on one simple rule that works for length, mass and capacity alike. ⭐ The Golden Rule of Converting Going from a BIGGER unit to a SMALLER unit, you MULTIPLY (the number gets bigger). Going from a SMALLER unit to a BIGGER unit, you DIVIDE (the number gets smaller).
concepts.example: Why does this make sense? A metre is a big unit and a centimetre is a small unit. It takes many small centimetres to make up one big metre, so when you swap to the smaller unit the number must grow larger – that is why…

concepts.title: Choosing Units and Solving Problems
concepts.explanation: Before you measure anything, you must choose a sensible unit. You would not measure the distance between Mogadishu and Kismaayo in millimetres – the number would be enormous and useless. Nor would you measure the width of a coin in metres. Choosing the right unit keeps the number small enough to be helpful and large enough to be exact. Measure the length of a classroom in metres.
concepts.example: Measure the length of a pencil in centimetres.

methods.title: How to measure length with a ruler
methods.example: A pencil starts at the 0 mark and its tip reaches the 12th large mark and 4 small marks past it. How long is the pencil?
steps: Put the 0 mark of the ruler at one end of the object.
Look at where the other end reaches.
Read the whole centimetres, then count the extra millimetre marks.
Write the length, for example 7 cm 3 mm or 7.3 cm.

methods.title: How to convert between units
methods.example: A ribbon is 45 cm long. How many millimetres is that?
steps: Decide if you are moving to a smaller unit or a bigger unit.
Smaller unit: multiply by 10, 100 or 1000.
Bigger unit: divide by 10, 100 or 1000.
Check the number changed in the direction you expected.

methods.title: How to solve a measurement problem
methods.example: A table is 1.8 m long. How many centimetres is that?
steps: Decide what you are looking for.
Convert every measurement to the same unit.
Add, subtract, multiply or divide as the problem needs.
Write the answer with the correct unit.

methods.title: Convert kg to g
methods.example: A bag of apples has a mass of 2.5 kg. How many grams is that?
steps: Solution: Kilograms to grams means multiply by 1000.
Check the convert kg to g result carefully.

methods.title: Convert ml to l
methods.example: A jug holds 3500 ml of water. How many litres is that?
steps: Solution: Millilitres to litres means we go from a smaller unit to a bigger unit, so we divide by 1000.
The number got smaller, which is correct.

methods.title: Find a total length
methods.example: One ribbon is 45 cm long and another is 75 cm long. What is their total length?
steps: Solution: Both are already in centimetres, so no conversion is needed.
Add them: 45 cm + 75 cm = 120 cm.
As a check, 120 cm is more than either ribbon on its own, which makes sense for a total.

workedExamples.title: Read a length in cm and mm
workedExamples.prompt: A pencil starts at the 0 mark and its tip reaches the 12th large mark and 4 small marks past it. How long is the pencil?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The large marks are centimetres and the small marks are millimetres. The tip reaches 12 whole centimetres and 4 extra millimetres, so the pencil is 12 cm 4 mm, which we can also write as 12.4 cm.

workedExamples.title: Convert cm to mm
workedExamples.prompt: A ribbon is 45 cm long. How many millimetres is that?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: We are going from a bigger unit (cm) to a smaller unit (mm), so we multiply. There are 10 mm in each cm, so 45 × 10 = 450 mm.

workedExamples.title: Convert m to cm
workedExamples.prompt: A table is 1.8 m long. How many centimetres is that?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Metres to centimetres means multiply by 100. So 1.8 × 100 = 180 cm. The number grew bigger, which is what we expect when moving to a smaller unit.

workedExamples.title: Convert kg to g
workedExamples.prompt: A bag of apples has a mass of 2.5 kg. How many grams is that?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Kilograms to grams means multiply by 1000. So 2.5 × 1000 = 2500 g.

workedExamples.title: Convert ml to l
workedExamples.prompt: A jug holds 3500 ml of water. How many litres is that?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Millilitres to litres means we go from a smaller unit to a bigger unit, so we divide by 1000. So 3500 ÷ 1000 = 3.5 l. The number got smaller, which is correct.

workedExamples.title: Find a total length
workedExamples.prompt: One ribbon is 45 cm long and another is 75 cm long. What is their total length?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both are already in centimetres, so no conversion is needed. Add them: 45 cm + 75 cm = 120 cm. As a check, 120 cm is more than either ribbon on its own, which makes sense for a total.

workedExamples.title: Find a difference in mass
workedExamples.prompt: A bag of flour has a mass of 2.5 kg and a bag of sugar has a mass of 1.75 kg. How much heavier is the flour?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both are in kilograms, so subtract: 2.5 kg − 1.75 kg = 0.75 kg. The flour is 0.75 kg heavier than the sugar. The answer is smaller than the flour’s mass, which makes sense for a difference.

workedExamples.title: Convert then compare
workedExamples.prompt: Jug A holds 2.5 litres and Jug B holds 2750 millilitres. Which jug holds more?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The units differ, so first make them match. Change 2.5 l into millilitres: 2.5 × 1000 = 2500 ml. Now compare: 2500 ml is less than 2750 ml, so Jug B holds more. Converting to one unit first made the comparison easy and safe.

workedExamples.title: A division problem with units
workedExamples.prompt: A piece of string is 1.2 m long. How many 15 cm pieces can be cut from it?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: First convert so the units match. Change 1.2 m into centimetres: 1.2 × 100 = 120 cm. Now divide by the length of each piece: 120 ÷ 15 = 8 pieces. Always convert to a single unit before dividing.

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: A ribbon is 45 cm long. A second ribbon is 30 cm long. What is the total length in millimetres?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Order from shortest to longest: 0.5 m, 40 cm, 600 mm.
workedExamples.solution: 40 cm (0.4 m), then 0.5 m (50 cm), then 600 mm (60 cm), so order: 40 cm, 0.5 m, 600 mm.

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Sarah measured her bedroom as 4 m 25 cm. Write this length in centimetres only.

activities.title: Estimate-Then-Measure Length Hunt
activities.materials: a ruler or tape measure, paper, a pencil, and five objects (a pencil, a spoon, a book, your foot, a table).
steps: Write down each object, then estimate its length in centimetres before you measure.
Measure each object with the ruler, starting carefully at the 0 mark.
Write the real length next to your estimate, in cm and mm.
Work out the difference between your estimate and the real length for each object.
Were your later estimates closer than your first ones? Estimating gets better with practice.

activities.title: Kitchen Scale Challenge (Mass)
activities.materials: a kitchen scale if you have one, and objects such as an apple, a book, a cup of rice, and a bag of sugar.
steps: Hold each object and guess whether it is measured better in grams or kilograms.
Estimate the mass of each object, using the 1 kg sugar bag as your benchmark.
Weigh each object and write down the real mass.
Find one object with a mass close to 100 g and one close to 500 g.
Try to find a big light object and a small heavy object to show that size is not the same as mass.

activities.title: Water Pouring Investigation (Capacity)
activities.materials: a measuring jug, water, and several containers (a cup, a bottle, a bowl, a pot).
steps: Estimate how much water each container holds, in millilitres or litres.
Fill each container, then pour the water into the measuring jug and read it at eye level.
Write the real capacity next to your estimate.
Put the containers in order from the smallest capacity to the largest.
Find two differently shaped containers that hold the same amount of water.

activities.title: The Conversion Card Game
activities.materials: paper cards. On some write a measurement (for example 3 kg, 250 cm, 2 l), and on others write its match (3000 g, 2.5 m, 2000 ml).
steps: Make at least eight matching pairs, mixing length, mass and capacity.
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down in a grid.
Turn over two cards. If they are equal measurements, you keep the pair.
If they do not match, turn them back and let the next player try.
The player with the most matched pairs wins. Say the golden rule aloud each turn.

activities.title: Build a Recipe (Family Task)
activities.materials: a family member, and a simple recipe such as anjero or bariis.
steps: Read the recipe together and list every measurement it uses.
Sort the measurements into length, mass and capacity.
Convert one mass into grams and one capacity into millilitres.
Help measure the ingredients using a scale and a measuring jug.
Talk about why the cook must measure carefully and not just guess.

activities.title: Masjid Distance Project (Investigation)
activities.materials: a tape measure or a metre stick, and a safe, measured path such as a courtyard.
steps: Measure the length of ten of your normal steps in metres and centimetres.
Work out the length of one step by dividing the total by ten.
Count your steps along a known path, then estimate its length using your step size.
If you can, measure the path with the tape measure and compare it to your estimate.
Convert your final distance into centimetres to see how large the number becomes.

rules.title: Sometimes a measurement is given in one
rules.text: Sometimes a measurement is given in one unit, but we need it in another. Changing a measurement from one unit to another is called converting. The whole idea rests on one simple rule that works for length, mass and capacity alike.

rules.title: ⭐ The Golden Rule of Converting Going
rules.text: ⭐ The Golden Rule of Converting Going from a BIGGER unit to a SMALLER unit, you MULTIPLY (the number gets bigger). Going from a SMALLER unit to a BIGGER unit, you DIVIDE (the number gets smaller).

rules.title: Say the golden rule aloud: smaller unit
rules.text: Say the golden rule aloud: smaller unit means multiply, bigger unit means divide.

rules.title: Next you will build on these measuring
rules.text: Next you will build on these measuring skills in your work with number, money and shape. Measurement appears everywhere: you will use lengths when you study perimeter and area, and you will use your converting skills whenever numbers grow into the thousands. Prerequisite check: Can you multiply and divide by 10, 100 and 1000? Can you read a ruler from the 0 mark? Can you say the golden rule of converting? If yes, you are ready. If not, spend a little more time here first, and ask your AI tutor to review anything that still feels shaky.

terms: How long, tall or far something is
How much liquid a container holds
Change from one unit to another
The curved top of a liquid in a jug
A known size used to help estimate
commonMistakes: Line up the 0 mark with the object
Smaller unit multiply, bigger unit divide
Convert to the same unit first
Read at eye level at the meniscus
Always write cm, g or ml after the number

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Sarah measured her bedroom as 4 m 25 cm. Write this length in centimetres only.
A carpenter cuts a 2 m board into 8 equal pieces. How long is each piece in centimetres?
A recipe needs 250 ml of milk, but the measuring cup holds 1 l. What fraction of the cup should be filled?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 250 ml out of 1000 ml = 250 ÷ 1000 = one quarter (1/4) of the cup.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A box holds 24 dates, and each date has a mass of 15 g. What is the total mass in grams, and in kilograms?
Order from least to greatest: 2000 ml, 2.5 l, 0.2 l.
Explain the key idea in Choosing Units and Solving Problems.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Before you measure anything, you must choose a sensible unit. You would not measure the distance between Mogadishu and Kismaayo in millimetres – the number would be enormous and useless. Nor would you measure the width of a coin in metres. Choosing the right unit keeps the number small enough to be helpful and large enough to be exact. Measure the length of a classroom in metres.

selfAssessment: I can measure length using millimetres, centimetres and metres, and read a ruler correctly.
I can measure mass using grams and kilograms, and read a scale.
I can measure capacity using millilitres and litres, and read a measuring jug.
I can estimate a measurement first, then measure carefully to check.
I can convert between units: mm and cm, cm and m, g and kg, ml and l.
I can choose the most sensible unit for measuring different objects.
I can solve real-world measurement problems using addition, subtraction, multiplication and division.
I can compare and order objects by their length, mass or capacity.
